perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware and that the Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that it is aware that this agreement can be executed in multiple counterparts, each of which shall be deemed to be an original, but all of which shall constitute one and the same Agreement. The Receiving Party and its Representatives have been advised that the United States securities laws prohibit any person having non-public material information about a company from purchasing or selling securities of that company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the disclosure about insider trading to the 'Counterparts' section buries it where it is unlikely to be noticed or understood. This weakens its enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits insider trading. By placing the disclosure in an obscure section, the contract makes it more difficult to demonstrate that the Receiving Party was properly informed of these prohibitions, potentially weakening the legal protection against insider trading violations. The placement fails to ensure the Receiving Party fully understands its obligations under US securities laws, including 17 CFR § 240.10b-5.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text moves a critical disclosure about insider trading into a section concerning 'Counterparts,' which is a structural flaw that undermines the enforceability of the disclosure. This creates a contradiction because it fails to adequately inform the Receiving Party of their obligations under US securities laws, including the prohibitions outlined in 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, which addresses manipulative and deceptive devices in securities trading. By obscuring this information, the contract weakens the protection against insider trading violations, making it more difficult to demonstrate that the Receiving Party was properly informed, as per the Law Explanation. The disclosure's new placement does not ensure the Receiving Party fully understands their obligations.

perturbation.original_text: All notices and other communications required or permitted hereunder will be in writing and will be deemed to have been duly given when delivered in person or when dispatched by email or electronic facsimile transfer (with such facsimile confirmed in writing by mail simultaneously dispatched) to the Company at the address specified below:
perturbation.changed_text: All notices and other communications required or permitted hereunder will be in writing and will be deemed to have been duly given when delivered in person or when dispatched by email or electronic facsimile transfer (with such facsimile confirmed in writing by mail simultaneously dispatched) to the Company at the address specified below: The Receiving Party agrees that, for a period of eighteen months from the date of this Agreement, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its affiliates, subject to Section 22 hereof, not to), directly or indirectly, (i) use the Confidential Information to divert or attempt to divert any business or customer of the Company or (ii) employ or solicit, or initiate contact for employment with, any (A) director, officer or other senior or key employee of the Company or (B) any other employee of the Company whom the Receiving Party meets during its evaluation of the possible Transaction or about whom the Receiving Party receives Confidential Information; provided , however , a general advertisement or other recruiting efforts not 2 specifically targeting any such employees of the Company shall not be considered a solicitation or unauthorized hiring.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the non-solicitation clause into the middle of the 'Notices' section obscures it, making it less likely to be noticed and understood. This weakens its enforceability, particularly if the Company later claims the Receiving Party violated the non-solicitation agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York law regarding non-solicitation agreements (implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing)
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By burying the non-solicitation clause in the middle of a section about 'Notices,' the contract acts in bad faith, potentially weakening the clause's enforceability. This undermines the reasonable expectations of the parties, which the implied covenant is designed to protect.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text buries the non-solicitation clause within the 'Notices' section, which introduces a structural flaw by obscuring a legally relevant obligation. The Law Explanation suggests this violates the implied covenant of good faith. The original text clearly separates the 'Notices' section from other contractual obligations, but the changed text merges them, potentially weakening the enforceability of the non-solicitation clause. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both state "Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.". By burying this clause in an unrelated section, it creates an argument that the company is not acting in good faith in presenting the contract. This structural flaw affects the clarity and enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement, creating a legal contradiction according to the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: The Company agrees (a) that the meeting date for its 2012 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 28, 2012) will be held no earlier than thirty-one days after the first anniversary of the Company’s 2011 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 29, 2011) and (b) to provide the Receiving Party a copy of the press release announcing the date of its 2012 annual meeting on the date such press release is publicly issued.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company agrees (a) that the meeting date for its 2012 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 28, 2012) will be held no earlier than thirty-one days after the first anniversary of the Company’s 2011 annual stockholders meeting (with respect to the Company’s fiscal year ended January 29, 2011) and (b) to provide the Receiving Party a copy of the press release announcing the date of its 2012 annual meeting on the date such press release is publicly issued. Without the Company’s prior written consent, which may be withheld by the Company in its sole discretion, the Receiving Party shall not (and shall cause its Representatives not to) initiate, other than through the Company’s financial and legal advisors or such other persons, as designated by the Company in writing, any (a) communication concerning the Confidential Information, (b) requests for meetings with management of the Company in connection with the possible Transaction or other transaction between the parties or (c) communication relating to the business of the Company or the possible Transaction, in each case, with any officer, director or employee of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the communications restriction clause to the section discussing the date of the 2012 stockholders meeting is an unusual and misleading placement. This makes it harder for the Receiving Party to understand the restrictions on communication, potentially leading to unintentional violations and weakened enforceability for the Company.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 14(a) and Rule 14a-9
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 14(a) and Rule 14a-9 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 regulate proxy solicitations. By burying the communication restrictions clause within the section concerning the stockholders meeting date, the contract makes it more difficult to ensure that all communications related to the meeting are clear and not misleading, as required by Rule 14a-9. This increases the risk of violating federal securities law, as the placement does not facilitate proper and transparent communication.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.14a-9 - False or misleading statements. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.14a-9 - False or misleading statements. CFR prev | next § 240.14a-9 False or misleading statements. (a) No solicitation subject to this regulation shall be made by means of any proxy statement , form of proxy , notice of meeting or other communication, written or oral, containing any statement which, at the time and in the light of the circumstances under which it is made, is false or misleading with respect to any material fact, or which omits to state any material fact necessary in order to make the statements therein not false or misleading or necessary to correct any statement in any earlier communication with respect to the solicitation of a proxy for the same meeting or subject matter which has become false or misleading. (b) The fact that a proxy statement , form of proxy or other soliciting material has been filed with or examined by the Commission shall not be deemed a finding by the Commission that such material is accurate or complete or not false or misleading, or that the Commission has passed upon the merits of or approved any statement contained therein or any matter to be acted upon by security holders. No representation contrary to the foregoing shall be made. (c) No nominee, nominating shareholder or nominating shareholder group, or any member thereof, shall cause to be included in a registrant 's proxy materials, either pursuant to the Federal proxy rules, an applicable state or foreign law provision, or a registrant 's governing documents as they relate to including shareholder nominees for director in a registrant 's proxy materials, include in a notice on Schedule 14N ( § 240.14n-101 ), or include in any other related communication, any statement which, at the time and in the light of the circumstances under which it is made, is false or misl
perturbation.justification: The changed text buries a communications restriction within a section discussing the stockholders meeting date. This placement obscures the restrictions, increasing the likelihood of unintentional violations. This contradicts 17 CFR § 240.14a-9, which states that no solicitation shall be made by means of any communication containing any statement which is misleading with respect to any material fact. By making the communication restrictions harder to find, the modified text increases the risk of misleading communications, creating a legal contradiction as it undermines the clarity and transparency required by securities law.
